chunk_summary: Guidelines for transfusion thresholds and overload prevention in severe anemia among SAM patients, emphasizing careful monitoring, specific triggers, and diuretic use in transfusion-associated volume overload.
learning_objectives: Understand transfusion thresholds for severe anemia in SAM patients based on hemoglobin levels and clinical signs.
Learn monitoring protocols during transfusion to prevent circulatory overload and manage complications.
Differentiate diuretic use for transfusion-associated overload versus nutritional edema in SAM.
Recognize the role of early transfusion in suspected shock with anemia and sepsis.
key_facts: Transfusion triggers: hemoglobin <4 g/dL or respiratory distress with hemoglobin 4–6 g/dL.
Use 10 mL/kg of packed red cells or fresh whole blood infused slowly over ~3 hours.
Monitor pulse, respiratory rate, perfusion, and liver size during transfusion to detect overload signs.
Diuretics like furosemide are used only for transfusion-associated overload, not nutritional edema.
Indian guidance favors early transfusion over repeated fluid boluses in suspected shock with anemia and sepsis.
reference_quote: Indian training materials define transfusion triggers as hemoglobin <4 g/dL (or PCV <12%), or respiratory distress when hemoglobin is 4–6 g/dL.
document_key_topics: Diuretic use in transfusion-related volume overload
Early transfusion in shock with anemia and sepsis
